title: Pan con tomate
subtitle: Catalan grilled bread rubbed with ripe tomato and olive oil.
excerpt: Catalan breakfast bread, toasted hard, rubbed with a halved tomato until the cut side is gone, then drenched in olive oil and finished with salt. Five minutes from cold bread to a plate. The whole thing turns on the quality of the tomato and the bread.
sourceNotes:
Catalan and Balearic home cookery, where pa amb tomàquet is the workaday breakfast and the everyday accompaniment to cured meats and cheese. Documented in nineteenth-century Catalan cookery and rooted in earlier practice of moistening dry bread with tomato pulp.

The technique is sometimes called pan tumaca in Aragon and Murcia. The dish moved north and south through Spain and into the tapas canon over the twentieth century.

The ratio of bread to tomato to oil is the whole recipe. Use a ripe summer tomato, a robust loaf, and a Spanish or Italian extra-virgin olive oil with peppery character.

content.text: Pan con tomate is a slice of bread rubbed with the cut side of a halved tomato until the cut side is gone. Salt, oil, eat. The whole thing takes five minutes and the result is the breakfast that holds up most Catalan mornings.

The bread has to be a robust country loaf with a structure that survives the rub. A baguette will do at a pinch. Soft sandwich bread breaks down into pulp. The tomato has to be ripe enough that the pulp comes away easily; an unripe tomato just scratches the surface. The oil has to be a decent extra-virgin with peppery character; a flat olive oil leaves a flat plate.
The traditional fruit is the
ramallet
, a Catalan winter-keeping tomato that holds firm flesh and sweet flavour for months. Any ripe summer tomato off the vine works just as well.
What you need

items.name: Bread
items.prepNote: from a country loaf or baguette, cut 1.5 cm thick

content.text: You also need a cast-iron griddle for the bar marks, or a heavy frying pan. The bread can go under a hot grill if neither is to hand.
Method
Toast the bread hard

Set a griddle pan over a high heat for three minutes until smoking. Lay the {{bread}} of bread across the bars and press lightly. Toast for ninety seconds on one side, then turn and give the second side a minute. You want dark bar marks and a crisp surface that will not collapse when you rub it. If the bread is softer than ideal, give it longer.
Rub the garlic, optional
If you want a savoury edge, take half {{garlic}} of garlic and run the cut face once or twice across the hot bread. Once. The garlic should be a hint, not a layer. Many Catalans skip it; ask first.
Rub the tomato

Halve {{tomato}} tomatoes across the equator, exposing the seed cavities. Take one half and rub the cut side hard against the toasted bread. The pulp and seeds press into the toast; the skin stays in your hand. Work in long strokes until the cut side is gone and the bread is sitting under a thin coat of pink pulp. One half does one slice; the other does the next.
Oil and salt

Drizzle {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil across the slices, working in a slow spiral so it lands evenly. Finish with a pinch of fine sea salt over each slice. Serve straight away, on the plate or in the hand.

attrs.intro: Pan con tomate has three failure modes and they all trace back to one of the three ingredients.

items.fix: Wait a day or two for it to ripen. A firm tomato slides across the toast without giving up any pulp, and you end up with scratched bread and pink fingers.
items.symptom: The tomato won't grip the bread.

items.fix: Cut the slices 1.5 cm thick and toast them harder than you think. The bread should crackle when you tap it before the tomato goes on.
items.cause: Toast was too soft, or bread too thin.
items.symptom: The bread turns soggy within a minute.

items.fix: Use a peppery extra-virgin from Spain or southern Italy. Mild oils have their place; not here.
items.cause: Olive oil is mild or stale.

items.fix: One light pass across the bread is enough. If you have rubbed harder, scrape some off with the back of a knife.
items.cause: Garlic rubbed more than once or pressed too hard.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting

content.text: Variations
Top with thin slices of serrano ham, manchego, or boquerones for a tapas plate. A few capers and a chopped anchovy work too. Restraint matters; the tomato and oil should still lead.

Out of season, when good tomatoes are not to be had, switch to a Catalan winter-keeping tomato if you can find one, or a hothouse tomato grated through the coarse holes of a box grater into a bowl and spooned on. Better than rubbing an underripe fruit across the bread.
Where this dish lives

Pan amb tomàquet is breakfast across Catalonia, the Balearics, and Valencia, and the bread of every casual lunch table. It sits next to coffee in the morning and next to a plate of jamón at midday. The dish is older than the recipe form suggests; rubbing dry bread with whatever was to hand is one of those gestures that predate written cookery.

Tomatoes only reached Spain in numbers in the 1600s. So the modern dish is newer than the idea behind it. Today it shows the easy style of Spanish home cooking. Three things, one minute, a plate worth slowing down for.
